cue: How do you urge someone on?

es.meaning: Come on!; that's it!; also 'exactly!' when agreeing.
es.regionNote: Quintessentially Mexican (with cousin ¡órale!). Spain urges with ¡venga! or ¡vamos!. The -le is the famous Mexican intensifier clitic (ándale, órale, échale, córrele).

it.meaning: Come on!; go on; also softening disbelief (ma dai = no way).
it.exampleTranslation: Come on, let's head out, it's late!

bridge.note: Both languages turned an imperative into pure encouragement, but they grabbed different verbs: Italian urges you with GIVE (dai), Mexico with WALK (ándale) — motion for motion's sake, wearing the untranslatable -le that Mexican Spanish bolts onto commands. Neither is understood literally by anyone, which is what makes them markers rather than verbs.

interference.es_to_it: 'Cammina!' as encouragement reads as an order to literally walk — the Italian nudge is dai. And dai doubles as disbelief: ma dai!
interference.it_to_es: '¡Da!' means nothing in Spanish. Mexico walks you forward: ándale (Spain: venga). The -le has no Italian counterpart — don't seek one.

review.notes: From Joe's Italian Matters marker list 2026-07-20 (discovery source only; all examples freshly authored). ES pairing by Claude, UNVERIFIED. The -le clitic note is a whole future entry (órale/échale/córrele family).
